title: Step 2 — Constraints & Budgets
summary: Turn requirements into numbers: latency budgets tied to human perception limits, cost-per-task, token/context limits, and privacy/on-device constraints — the back-of-the-envelope math that sizes an agent.
analogy: A budget is a fence around your design space. Before you decorate a room you measure the walls; before you architect an agent you measure your milliseconds, dollars, and tokens.
body:
## First principles
Constraints are what make design *engineering* rather than wishful thinking. Two designs can both 'work' on paper; the one that fits the latency, cost, and token budgets is the real one. Estimation isn't about precision — it's about finding the **order of magnitude** that rules architectures in or out.

## Latency budget — anchor it to human perception
Nielsen Norman Group's three response-time limits are the canonical anchors:
- **0.1 s (100 ms)** — feels *instant*. Direct-manipulation feedback (a hover hint, a cursor-anchored suggestion) must land here.
- **1 s** — keeps the user's *flow of thought* unbroken. A short agent suggestion should resolve by ~1 s.
- **10 s** — the limit of *keeping attention*. Beyond this, stream partial results or show progress or the user leaves.

So: cheap local UI cues ≤100 ms; a model suggestion target ~1 s; any multi-step tool action must **stream** (see streaming-responses) to survive past 10 s. These numbers dictate where you put caching, on-device models, and prefetching.

## Decompose the latency
A single agent suggestion is a chain. Budget each hop:

`sense (capture screen/cursor) + ground (build context) + model (LLM call) + tool (act) + render`

If your total budget is 1 s and the LLM round-trip alone is 700 ms, sensing+grounding must be cheap (a11y tree read, not full OCR) and you must prefetch/predict. This is the math that forces on-device vs cloud splits.

## Cost-per-task
Estimate: `tokens_in + tokens_out` per step × steps per task × price/token. If a task is 5 ReAct steps × ~3k tokens × $X, you get a per-task cost. Multiply by daily active tasks → infra bill. High cost pushes you toward smaller models, caching, and fewer loop iterations.

## Token / context limits
Context windows are finite. A screen-reading agent could capture *megabytes* of accessibility tree + OCR text. You **cannot** dump it all in. Budget tokens: reserve room for system prompt, tools, and reply; the rest is your context budget. This is why the context pipeline (Step 4) must rank and compress.

## Privacy / on-device constraints
Some data must never leave the machine (screen contents, keystrokes). That's a hard constraint, not a preference. It forces: on-device capture, on-device redaction before any cloud call, and possibly an on-device small model for sensitive paths. Privacy budgets are as real as latency budgets.

## Back-of-the-envelope, worked
- Perception-instant cue: **≤100 ms** → must be local, no network.
- Model suggestion: **~1 s** → one cloud LLM call, small model, prefetched context.
- Context budget: window 200k tokens − 8k (system+tools) − 4k (reply) ≈ **188k** usable, but aim for ~4–8k of *ranked* context for latency/cost, not the max.
- Cost: 5 steps × 3k tokens ≈ 15k tokens/task → at scale, the line item that decides your model tier.

## Watch out for
- **Latency tail, not mean**: the p95 is what users feel; budget for it.
- **Token bloat**: raw screen captures explode context; compress early.
- **Forgotten privacy cost**: redaction and on-device inference add latency you must pre-budget.
diagram:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Latency budget ~1s
    S[Sense ≤150ms] --> G[Ground/context ≤150ms] --> M[Model call ~600ms] --> A[Tool/act ≤50ms] --> R[Render ≤50ms]
  end
  P[NN/g limits: 100ms instant · 1s flow · 10s attention] -.anchors.-> S
  B[Token budget: window − system − reply = usable] -.caps.-> G
  C[Cost: tokens × steps × price] -.bounds.-> M

examples.what: The 0.1s / 1s / 10s thresholds are the industry anchors for what 'fast enough' means; agent UIs inherit them directly.
Demos show multi-second screenshot→reason→click loops, which is exactly why such agents must stream progress and stay under the 10s attention limit per step.

drills.q: What are NN/g's three response-time limits and what does each govern?
drills.a: 0.1s = feels instant (direct feedback); 1s = unbroken flow of thought; 10s = limit of attention. Past 10s you must stream or show progress.

drills.q: Your suggestion latency budget is 1s and the LLM call is 700ms. What does that force in the rest of the design?
drills.a: Sensing and context-building must be cheap (read the a11y tree, not full OCR), and you must prefetch/predict context so only ~300ms is left for everything else.

drills.q: Why can't a screen-reading agent put its full captured context into the model?
drills.a: Raw a11y tree + OCR can be megabytes; the context window is finite and tokens cost latency and money, so the context must be ranked and compressed first.

teachItBack: Step two turns requirements into numbers: anchor latency to NN/g's 100ms/1s/10s perception limits, decompose the budget across sense-ground-model-act-render, then estimate cost-per-task and your token budget. Hard privacy constraints can force on-device capture and redaction before any cloud call. These numbers rule whole architectures in or out before you draw them.

sources.title: NN/g — Response Times: The 3 Important Limits
Hello Interview — System Design in a Hurry (estimation)

glossary.definition: A target end-to-end response time, decomposed across each processing hop, anchored to human perception thresholds.
Quick order-of-magnitude math (tokens, ms, dollars) used to rule architectures in or out without exact precision.
The token allowance left for retrieved/sensed context after reserving room for the system prompt, tools, and the model's reply.

quiz.q: Per NN/g, beyond which response time must you stream partial results or risk losing the user's attention?
quiz.explanation: 10 seconds is the limit for keeping a user's attention; past it you must show progress or stream.

quiz.q: Why does a tight latency budget often force an on-device step?
choices: On-device is always cheaper to build
Perception-instant cues (≤100ms) can't survive a network round-trip
It removes the need for guardrails
quiz.explanation: A 100ms 'instant' cue has no time for a network call, so that feedback must run locally.
